title: Disposable Email Domain Velocity Spike with High-Value Digital Goods
narrative: Fraudster registers multiple accounts using throwaway email services (mailinator.com, guerrillamail.com, 10minutemail.net) to bypass merchant fraud rules that flag repeat customers. Pattern emerges when P_emaildomain matches known disposable providers and multiple transactions occur within 72 hours targeting high-margin digital products (gift cards, gaming currency, software licenses). Transactions typically range $75-$250 to stay under manual review thresholds. Card diversity is highâ€”fraudster cycles through 8-12 stolen card numbers (distinct card1 values) all shipping to same addr1 or within 15km radius (dist1 <15). Transactions cluster in off-hours (txn_hour between 1-5 AM merchant local time, is_night=1) when fraud teams are understaffed. The disposable email never matches R_emaildomain, indicating no prior relationship. Card1_txn_count_24h shows 0-1 prior use, suggesting fresh test of stolen PANs. Pattern terminates abruptly after 18-36 hours when chargebacks begin surfacing.
indicators: P_emaildomain belongs to known disposable provider list (mailinator, 10minutemail, guerrillamail, tempmail, sharklasers)
6-15 transactions within 72-hour window all sharing same addr1 or dist1 <15km
ProductCD = 'C' or 'H' (digital goods) with TransactionAmt between $75-$250 per transaction
Each transaction uses distinct card1 value with card1_txn_count_24h â‰¤ 1 indicating fresh cards
P_emaildomain â‰  R_emaildomain (no historical email match)
Card1_amt_zscore > 2.5 indicating unusual spending pattern for those card numbers
summary: Multiple accounts created with disposable email domains (like mailinator or 10minutemail) place 6-15 orders for digital goods worth $75-$250 each within 72 hours, all shipping to the same address or within 15km, using a different credit card for each transaction during overnight hours.
reasoning: Criminals use throwaway emails to evade repeat-customer flags while testing freshly stolen credit cards on high-margin digital products that can be quickly resold, clustering purchases at night when fraud monitoring is weakest and stopping once chargebacks start appearing.
indicator_explanations: Email comes from throwaway service designed to evade tracking and verification
Multiple purchases cluster from same location within three-day attack window
Targets high-value digital goods that can be instantly resold or monetized
Each transaction burns a different stolen card number used only once
Email address has no purchase history tied to any previous transactions
Activity happens during overnight hours when fraud monitoring is lightest
Purchase amounts are abnormally high compared to each card's typical use
